coredata.dc:title: Environment-friendly Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 photocatalysts: Remarkably enhanced photocatalytic tetracycline degradation in visible light
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Qu, Songying
Xiong, Yuhan
coredata.dc:description: The g-C3N4 was widely used in water purification recently. However, the further application was severely suppressed because of the rapid electron-hole recombination and low visible-light utilization efficiency. Herein, we fabricated the three-component Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 nanocomposites which effectively enhanced the photodegradation of tetracycline (TC) under visible light irritation (～93.4% in 60 min), compared with Ag/g-C3N4 (∼76.1%), CDots/g-C3N4 (∼63.8%) and g-C3N4 (∼44.2%). The morphology, structural and compositional information of Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 was observed as well as the optical and electric properties, and the photocatalytic mechanism was revealed in detail. The addition of CDots could effectively increase light absorption efficiency, accelerate electron transfer and separation, and act as an electron-reservoir center. The presence of Ag had increased electron density via SPR effect, further enhancing the photocatalytic performance. The Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 also showed great stability and reusability with ignorable change in five recycle. Besides, the toxicity assessment proved the good biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity of Ag/CDots/g-C3N4. The study indicated that the successful synthesis of Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 paved way to water purification.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ag
CDots
Synergistic effect
Tetracycline
Photocatalysis
Cytotoxicity

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical and electric properties toxicity assessment

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Key Laboratory of Groundwater Resources & Environment, Ministry of Education, Jilin University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by Key Laboratory of Groundwater Resources & Environment, Ministry of Education, Jilin University .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM (a), HRTEM (b) and SEM (c) images and EDS mapping (d–f) of the as-prepared Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 photocatalyst.

XRD patterns (a) of CDots, g-C3N4, CDots/g-C3N4, Ag/g-C3N4 and Ag/CDots/g-C3N4. XPS spectra Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 nanocomposites (b) and high-resolution XPS spectra of N 1 s (c), C 1s (d), O 1s (e) and Ag 3d (f) on Ag/CDots/g-C3N4.
Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherm and pore size distribution curve of Ag/CDots/g-C3N4.

UV–vis DRS spectra of all the as-prepared nanocomposites (a); band gaps values, estimated from the plotted curve of (αhυ)1/2 vs hυ (b); XPS valence band spectrum of pure g-C3N4 and Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 (c); band structure diagram of Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 (d).
UV–vis–NIR absorption spectrum of CDots (a); PL spectra (b), Transient photo-current response (c), Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) (d) of g-C3N4 and Ag/CDots/g-C3N4.

Dark adsorption (a), Visible light photodegradation (b) and Langmuir-Hinshelwood model fitting (c) for the TC degradation over prepared catalysts; Recycling runs of photocatalytic activity of the Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 nanocomposites (d).

Quenching experiment on ROSs during the photocatalytic degradation of TC over Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 (a); DMPO spin-trapping ESR spectra (b); Schematic photocatalytic mechanism for the Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 nanocomposites under visible light irradiation (c).
Relative viability of Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) (a) and Escherichia coli (E. coli) (b) after 24 h incubation of H2O and Ag/CDots/g-C3N4.

head.ce:title: Environment-friendly Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 photocatalysts: Remarkably enhanced photocatalytic tetracycline degradation in visible light

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Environmental Science & Engineering Research Center, Harbin Institute of Technology, Shenzhen Graduate School, Shenzhen, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Groundwater Resources and Environment, Ministry of Education, Jilin University, Changchun, 130021, Jilin, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Groundwater Resources and Environment

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Environmental Science & Engineering Research Center, Harbin Institute of Technology, Shenzhen Graduate School, Shenzhen, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The g-C3N4 was widely used in water purification recently. However, the further application was severely suppressed because of the rapid electron-hole recombination and low visible-light utilization efficiency. Herein, we fabricated the three-component Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 nanocomposites which effectively enhanced the photodegradation of tetracycline (TC) under visible light irritation (～93.4% in 60 min), compared with Ag/g-C3N4 (∼76.1%), CDots/g-C3N4 (∼63.8%) and g-C3N4 (∼44.2%). The morphology, structural and compositional information of Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 was observed as well as the optical and electric properties, and the photocatalytic mechanism was revealed in detail. The addition of CDots could effectively increase light absorption efficiency, accelerate electron transfer and separation, and act as an electron-reservoir center. The presence of Ag had increased electron density via SPR effect, further enhancing the photocatalytic performance. The Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 also showed great stability and reusability with ignorable change in five recycle. Besides, the toxicity assessment proved the good biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity of Ag/CDots/g-C3N4. The study indicated that the successful synthesis of Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 paved way to water purification.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracycline (TC) has been widely used as a prevalent antibiotic to treat infections and causes numerous concern because of its bio-refractory nature in wastewater treatment [1–4]. Semiconductor photocatalysis was an effective way to treat TC, such as TiO2, ZnO, CdS, Bi2WO6 and g-C3N4 etc [5–9]. Among them, the g-C3N4 was extremely noticeable due to its nonmetal, low-cost and environmentally friendly properties [10–12]. The relatively low band gap energy, high valence band and conduction band positions of g-C3N4 also made it become a promising photocatalyst [10,13]. However, low visible light efficiency and rapid electron/hole recombination extremely restricted its further application [8,14,15]. It was necessary to optimize the photocatalytic properties of g-C3N4.

In order to solve these defects, several modifiers could be added into g-C3N4 to enhance the photocatalytic activity and conductivity, such as the carbon nanodots (CDots) and Ag nanodots [5,16]. Carbon nanodots (CDots), a novel graphite particles less than 10 nm in diameter, always served as a co-catalyst for semiconductor because of its up-conversion ability, low toxicity and high biocompatibility [17]. The CDots not only showed unprecedented photo-induced electron transfer properties, but also contributed to the visible light responses of semiconductor, and the CDots could act as an excellent electron-reservoir center during photocatalytic reaction [18,19]. The conjugated π structure of the CDots played a vital role in photocatalytic reactions [10]. The Surface Plasmon Resonance (SPR) effect of Ag could enhance light absorption, generate hot electrons and increase electron density under visible light [20–22]. It was reasonable to regard the Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 as a high-efficiency photocatalysts, which could utilize visible light sufficiently and has efficient charge separation.

The increasing applications of nanomaterials has triggered numerous environmental, health and safety problems recently [18,23]. Some nanoparticles would affect the genes expression in zebrafish embryos, others could inhibit seedling growth of different Arabidopsis thaliana, or show cytotoxicity to human lung cells (∼80 μg mL−1) and decrease the cell proliferation and viability of human keratinocytes [18,23–25]. Therefore, predicting the potential harm during environmental modification and evaluating the possible toxity of nanomaterials were essential.

Based on the above analyses, it was deserved to combine g-C3N4 with CDots and Ag in order to obtain an efficient photocatalysts. Besides, the phase, morphologies, structures, optical and electric properties of the prepared catalysts were investigated in detail. The synergistic effect between Ag, CDots and g-C3N4 was investigated and the possible photocatalytic mechanism was proposed. The possible toxicity of Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 was evaluated via the bactericidal experiments with Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) and Escherichia coli (E. coli) as models.

All chemicals were used directly without further purification. The Ag quantum dots and CDots were prepared by a facile oil bath reduction and a typical electrochemical method as previously reported, respectively [10,20]. Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 nanocomposites were prepared via a one-step synthetic method. 10 g urea dissolved into 5 mL CDots aqueous solution and 5 mL Ag hexane solution with vigorous stirring. After dried at 60 °C under vacuum, the mixture was transferred into a crucible with cover and heated to 550 °C at a rate of 5 °C for 3 h in a muffle furnace. The final obtained powder was washed with ethanol and ultrapure water for three times, dried at 60 °C under vacuum. The pure g-C3N4, Ag/g-C3N4 and CDots/g-C3N4 can be obtained respectively in the same way via adding nothing, Ag and CDots.

Phase composition was determined by the powder X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8, Germany) with a Cu Kα radiation. XPS was recorded with a VG ESCALAB210 (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) using an Al Kα (h = 1486.6 eV) radiation. The morphology and crystal structure of resultant catalysts were characterized via Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM, JEOL JSM-7800 F) equipped with an energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscope (EDS), Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM, via a 200 kV FEI-Tecnai F20, USA) and High-Resolution TEM (HRTEM). All spectra mentioned above were calibrated against the C 1s signal (BE = 284.6 eV). UV–Vis diffuse reflection spectra (DRS) of the resultant were obtained using a Lambda 750 spectrophotometer (Perking Elmer) in the wavelength range of 200–800 nm. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured with a luminescence spectrometer (Cary Eclipse, Varian Medical Systems, USA). The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface and pore structure were determined by N2 adsorption at 77 K using an ASAP 2020 automatic analyzer (Micromeritics Instrument, USA). Transient time-resolved photoluminescence decay measurements were carried out by a FL920 luminescence spectrometer with an excitation wavelength of 337 nm. Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra were performed on a Bruker model ESR JES-FA200 spectrometer by using the spin-trap reagent DMPO (5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide, Sigma Chemical Co.) in water (for hydroxyl radical) and methanol (for superoxide radical). The UV–Vis absorption spectra (ABS) were collected on a UV-2800 spectrophotometer (SDPTOP, China).

The transient photo-current and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were performed on a CHI-660e workstation (Shanghai, China) using a three-electrode configuration with a Ag/AgCl electrode as reference electrode and a Pt plate as counter electrode. The working electrode was prepared by spin-coating the as-prepared catalysts onto an FTO glass in 2800 rpm. 0.1 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution was used as the electrolyte.

The visible light source was provided by a 150 W Xenon lamp with an UV–IR cut-off filter to remove UV (<420 nm) and NIR (>780 nm) light and the spectrum was shown in Fig. S1. Adding 0.1 g photocatalysts into 100 mL TC (30 mg L–1) aqueous solution. The TC aqueous solution was stirred in the dark for 1 h before to make sure achieved adsorption/desorption equilibrium. The reactant was irradiated by the visible light, sampled a given volume of suspension regularly and then filtered by a 0.45 μm membrane during the irradiation. The filtered mixture was detected by an Agilent 1100 HPLC which was equipped with a 4.6 × 250 mm, 5 μm Venusil HILIC column, and the wavelength of the UV detector was set at 356 nm. The mobile phase (v/v) consisted of 0.02 M acetic acid (90%) and acetonitrile (10%) at a flow rate of 1.0 mL min–1. To make sure the stability and the repeatability of photocatalysts, the identical experiment was repeated for 5 times. The dissolved organic carbon (DOC) of TC was measured by a total organic carbon analyzer (SSM-5000 A, Shimadzu Company).

Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) and Escherichia coli (E. coli) were used to evaluate the cytotoxicity of Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 nanocomposites via observing cell growth inhibition. The procedure was referred to other paper [26]. Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 treated all the bacteria cultures for 18 h, then the treated bacteria cultures were plated on the Mueller-Hinton agar (MHA) and cultivated at 37 °C for 24 h. It seen as bacteria death if the treated cultures can’t form bacteria colonies, the number of colonies was recorded.

In Fig. 1 a, the TEM image of Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 showed a typical nanosheet shape of g-C3N4 which was existed with Ag and CDots in homogeneous dispersion, suggesting a close combination between g-C3N4 with Ag and CDots [10]. Ag and CDots can be further observed in the HRTEM image of Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 (Fig. 1b). Ag showed a lattice fringes with lattice spacing of 0.238 nm which was corresponding to the (111) plane [21]. The lattice spacing of CDots can be indentified as 0.212 nm, matching well with the (101) crystal plane of graphitic carbon [10]. The SEM image illustrated the nanosheet shape of nanocomposites (Fig. 1c). Determined by EDX, the images (Fig. 1d–f) depicted that the hybrid contained C, N, Ag with a uniform distribution. All the information mentioned indicted that Ag and CDots were combined with g-C3N4 successfully and equably dispersed on the surface of g-C3N4. The intimate contact was conductive to electron transfer [27].

The structure of Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 can also be illustrated by XRD patterns (Fig. 2 a). As shown in Fig. 2a, the XRD patterns of the CDots reflected three prominent peaks. Among them, the peak at 26° was corresponding to the (002) planes of graphite, and the other two peaks emerged at 22.59° and 18.20° were referring to the amorphous carbon and (103) planes (belongs to hexagonal carbon) [28]. Both g-C3N4 and CDots/g-C3N4 showed two characteristic peaks. The weaker one at 13.1° was corresponding to (100) crystal, while the stronger one at ∼27.5° was corresponding to (002) crystal, which were consistent with previous reports [10]. The strong diffraction peaks indicated that g-C3N4 and CDots/g-C3N4 were highly crystalline, which meant the existence of CDots can hardly change the structure of g-C3N4 because of the low contents, relative low diffraction intensity and fine particle sizes of CDots [29]. After adding Ag, different peaks appeared in Ag/g-C3N4 and Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 (labeled by black mark). The results showed that Ag combined with g-C3N4 successfully. No changes appeared in the XRD diffraction peaks of g-C3N4 after the addition of CDots or/and Ag. This meant that the CDots and Ag were only deposited on the surfaces of g-C3N4, rather than entering into the lattice internal and influencing the growth of g-C3N4 [6,18]. No extra characteristic peaks from possible impurities could be observed, which might indicate the high purity and single phase of the as prepared composites.

XPS was conducted to find out the chemical state of the Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 and prove the existence of Ag and CDots. The survey spectrum (Fig. 2b) depicted that the sample contained the elements of C 1s, Ag 3d, N 1s and O 1s. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of N 1s, C 1 s, O 1s, Ag 3d were demonstrated in Fig. 2c–f. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. 2c) showed five peaks at 398.5, 399.1, 400.0, 401.0 and 406.8 eV. The peaks at 398.5 and 401.0 eV were identified as CNC and quaternary N bonded to three carbon atoms in the aromatic cycles, respectively [10,30]. The peaks at 399.1 and 400.0 eV were corresponding to the tertiary N bonded to carbon atoms in the form of N–(C)3 [10,16]. The peak centered at 406.8 eV corresponds to the π excitations [10,16]. Fig. 2d displaced the C 1s spectrum, which can be divided into five peaks centered at 284.6, 285.3, 286.1, 288.2, 289.2 eV. The major C peak at 284.6 eV was assigned to graphitic carbon, and the subordinate C peak at 288.2 eV and 289.2 eV were attributed to the sp2-bonded carbon (NC)N [10,16]. Both C peaks located at 285.3 and 286.1 eV were assigned to COC [6,19]. The XPS signals of O 1 s were mainly composed of two peaks located at 532.0 and 532.8 eV, which were related to CO, OH bonds, respectively (Fig. 2e) [6]. Fig. 2f showed that the two distinct peaks centered at 368.2 and 374.1 eV belonging to the Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2, respectively [6]. According to the above XPS analysis results, the Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 photocatalyst was successfully fabricated, which was according with SEM, TEM and XRD measurements.

The surface area and pore structure of Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 were investigated by the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm (Fig. 3 ). Additionally, the pore size distribution of the Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 was determined using the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) method (inset in Fig. 3) [31,32]. According to IUPAC classification of mesoporous materials, the as-prepared samples with the hysteresis loop in the P/P0 range of 0.00–1.00 exhibited typical type IV isotherm [14]. As shown in Fig. 3, the mesopores in the size range of 2–50 nm were observed, which might be ascribed to ultrathin nanosheets of the sphere-like structure [33,34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and electric properties toxicity assessment
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 a compared the UV–Vis DRS spectra of as-prepared photocatalysts. The g-C3N4 could absorb UV light obviously but absorb visible light rarely, which accorded with the intrinsic absorption property of g-C3N4 [28]. After adding Ag and/or CDots, the absorption of g-C3N4 was increased effectively in the visible region with wavelength longer than 420 nm, which indicated that Ag and/or CDots enhanced the visible-light-absorption capacity of g-C3N4 [21,35,36]. Compared with pure g-C3N4, an obvious absorption appeared in the range of 410–460 nm in Ag/CDots/g-C3N4, which was the result of the strong SPR effect of the metallic Ag nanodots [37].

As shown in Fig. 4b, the optical band gaps (Eg) were calculated from the plots of (αhυ) versus (hυ) [38]. Thus, the band gaps of the four photocatalysts were determined to be 2.79, 2.80, 2.80, 2.81 eV, respectively. The similar Eg values indicated that the addition of Ag and CDots could hardly change the optical band gaps of g-C3N4. Fig. 4c showed the XPS valence band spectra of g-C3N4 and Ag/CDots/g-C3N4. The valence band energies (Ev) were both calculated at 1.87 eV. Accordingly, due to the equation Ec = Ev−Eg, the conduction band energies (Ec) of g-C3N4 and Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 were calculated to be −0.94, −0.93 eV, respectively [39,40]. Fig. 4d displays the band structure diagram of g-C3N4 and Ag/CDots/g-C3N4. Obviously, the addition of Ag and CDots would not change the band structure of g-C3N4.

As shown in Fig. 5 a, the as-prepared CDots showed a strong, broad and continuous absorption in the UV, visible and NIR regions. Therefore, the addition of CDots would help nanocomposites to utilize solar energy and enhance the light absorption efficiency to generate electron–hole pairs [27]. Fig. 5a inset was the up-converted PL spectrum of CDots, which indicated that CDots could be excited by long-wavelength light in the range from 700 nm to 1000 nm and the emission wavelength was from 345 to 575 nm. The results showed CDots could absorb long-wavelength light and radiate short-wavelength light, which could be absorbed effectively by g-C3N4 (according to Fig. 4a). The character of CDots probably contributed to the multiphoton active process via transferring the excited electrons to the acceptor [19].

The PL specta of Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 and g-C3N4 were shown in Fig. 5b, both them had a same emission peak located at ∼480 nm but the peak intensity of Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 was lower than that of g-C3N4. The results proved that the introduction of Ag and CDots would decrease the PL intensity of nanocomposites, which indicated that the addition of Ag and CDots could enhance the separation of photo-generated electrons-holes pairs [6,7,28,37]. As depicted in Fig. S2, the transient PL decay curves of g-C3N4 and CDots/g-C3N4 showed the PL lifetime of ∼0.45 and ∼0.59 ns, respectively. The ns level of PL lifetime implied the singlet state nature of emission of the samples [14,34]. A loger PL lifetime of CDots/g-C3N4 than g-C3N4 indicated that more effective electron transfer operated from g-C3N4 to CDots. In addition, the PL lifetime of Ag/g-C3N4 was ∼0.26 ns longer than that of g-C3N4, indicating the recombination opportunity of photoelectron-hole pairs and prolonged the PL lifetime [32,41]. More importantly, the PL lifetime (∼0.94 ns) of the Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 further extended to ∼2.1 times longer than that of the g-C3N4. The longer lifetime indicated that more electrons were transferred across the interface since the effective separation of charge carriers can be obtained [31,33]. Consequently, much more pronounced charge separation and efficiency of the photocatalytic reaction were achieved in Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 system. The transient photocurrent was further used to conduct the responses of Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 and pure g-C3N4 (Fig. 5c) under intermittent visible-light irradiation. Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 showed much stronger transient photocurrent response, about three times larger than that of g-C3N4. The results demonstrated that CDots was an effective electric mediator for separating the photo-generated electrons-holes pairs, because the special conjugated π structure of CDots could act as electron acceptors and separation centers. And the presence of Ag also enhanced the electron density due to the SPR effect of metallic Ag nanodots [37]. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was used to investigated the process of electron transfer and separation (Fig. 5d). The semicircle radius for Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 was far less than pure g-C3N4, implying Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 had a higher electron-hole separation efficiency and a faster interfacial charge transfer [42]. In addition, the equivalent circuit (inset of Fig. 5d) of the devices was conducted to analyze the reaction mechanisms involved in the electrode process. Rs, Rct, Rother, Cdl and CPEother represent the series resistance of the system, the resistance of semiconductor depletion layer, the charge transfer resistance in Helmholtz layer, and the chemical capacitance, respectively [33,43]. The result of the transient photo-current response and EIS matched well with the PL analyses, which proved Ag and CDots could enhance interfacial change transfer rate and the separation of electron–hole pairs, and significantly improve photocatalytic activity [44].

The results of absorption in the dark were shown in Fig. 6 a. In comparison, the removal efficiency of Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 reached up to 18% in 30 min, which would contribute to follow-up reaction [18]. Other photocatalysts showed lower absorption efficiency under the same conditions. Under visible-light irradiation (Fig. 6b), the removal efficiencies of TC were in the order Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 > Ag/g-C3N4 > CDots/g-C3N4 > g-C3N4 under the identical conditions. As observed, the best performance appeared on Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 and the degradation efficiency achieved 93.4% in 60 min. The performance of introducing Ag or CDots alone was not obvious as the performance of ternary photocatalyst, because of the strongest light absorption and the excellent electron transfer capability of Ag/CDots/g-C3N4. To compare the photocatalytic activity quantitatively, the degradation kinetics were calculated via Langmuir-Hinshelwood model (Fig. 6c). The Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 showed the highest reaction rate constant (k value, 0.0408 min–1) than those of Ag/g-C3N4 (0.0237 min–1), CDots/g-C3N4 (0.0160 min–1) and pristine g-C3N4 (0.0070 min–1), indicting the combination of Ag and CDots was better than Ag or CDots alone. Fig. S3 presented the mineralization in the entire photocatalytic TC degradation process. As observed, the Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 exhibited the highest mineralization efficiency of TC (77.57%), which was much higher than other samples under the identical conditions. The Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 also showed great stability and reusability with ignorable change, from 93.4% at the first TC degradation to 86.7% at the fifth use, which indicated the as-prepared Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 was stable and reusable (Fig. 6d).

To reveal the photocatalytic mechanism of Ag/CDots/g-C3N4, scavenger-quenching experiments were conducted to investigate the reactive oxidation species (ROSs) included in the reaction (Fig. 7 a). The scavengers including sodium oxalate, Cr6+, isopropanol and TEMPOL were added to trap ROSs, corresponding to h+, e−, OH, O2 −, respectively [45]. As shown in Fig. 7a, TC degradation efficiency was decreased significantly after adding sodium oxalate, isopropanol or TEMPOL, which meant that h+, e−, O2 − were all the main ROSs in the Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 photocatalytic system. The appearance of O2 − was the result of combination between e− and adsorbed O2 on semiconductor photocatalyst surface, when the CB position was more negative than −0.046 eV (vs. NHE, the standard redox potential of O2/O2 −) [40]. The CB position of Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 was −0.93 eV as calculated before, which was more negative than −0.046 eV, so O2 − could generate smoothly. CDots could enhance the electron transfer and store electrons, then the electrons combined with O2 to form O2 −, so O2 − generated on the surface of CDots. Another ROSs, OH, was produced from the reaction between h+ and adsorbed H2O on the surface of Ag/CDots/g-C3N4, when the VB position was more positive than 2.3 eV (vs. NHE, the standard redox potential of OH/OH−) [46]. However, the VB position of Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 was 1.87 eV by detected, couldn’t meet the condition of OH generation, so nearly no OH appeared. The SPR effect of Ag could increase electron density in the photocatalytic system. In addition, the ESR spin-trap technique was used to further confirm the active species and reveal the photodegradation mechanism [14,31,34]. As shown in Fig. 7b, the signal of DMPO−O2 − can be found clearly. It indicated that the O2 − was produced in the Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 system, which contributed to the higher photocatalytic activity of Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 composites. The signal of DMPO−OH was not be found which was in agreement with the results of scavenger-quenching experiments (Fig. 7a).

Based on the above results and related literatures [10,16,20], the photocatalytic mechanism for Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 was proposed in Fig. 7c. At first, when exposured by visible-light, the CDots could absorb long-wavelength light and then transform to short-wavelength light. The short-wavelength light would be absorbed by g-C3N4. In this way, g-C3N4 could absorb more visible light and be further excited. After being excited, the electrons moved from VB to CB of g-C3N4 with the holes left on the VB of g-C3N4. Meanwhile, the Ag could also fully absorb visible light and occur SPR effect to generate hot electrons [17,20]. Then, those hot electrons would be transferred to the CB of g-C3N4 to increase the electron density. Electron density further increased on the CB of g-C3N4. Then the electrons in the CB of g-C3N4 transferred to CDots through the contact interface not only because CDots could store electrons effectively but also the result of the excellent electronic conductivity of CDots and g-C3N4. Thus, h+ remained in the VB of g-C3N4 and e− concentrated in CDots, the spatial separation achieved. The recombination of electron–hole pairs would be avoided due to the spatial separation. Finally, the organic pollutants were absorbed by Ag/CDots/g-C3N4, and oxidation and reduction occurred on the surface of g-C3N4 and CDots respectively. Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 nanocomposites had an efficient photocatalytic activity as the result of synergistic effects of Ag, CDots and g-C3N4.

To predict the potential harm during environmental modification, the possible toxity of Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 was tested through the bactericidal experiments with S. aureus (Fig. 8 a) and E. coli (Fig. 8b) as models. In the medium containing 1 mg mL−1 Ag/CDots/g-C3N4, the treated cells grew normally and no inhibition can be observed. The results demonstrated that the Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 had good biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity.

It was proved by the results that the Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 were synthesized successfully via a facile method. Ag/CDots/g-C3N4 exhibited an excellent enhanced photocatalytic activity for TC degradation under visible light irradiation. The Ag and CDots played the crucial roles, which the CDots could not only increase light absorption efficiency of g-C3N4 but also be an excellent electron-transfer and electron-reservoir center to enhanced the TC degradation rate, and the Ag had the SPR effect under visible light to increase electron density. The results of toxicity assessment proved the good biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity of Ag/CDots/g-C3N4. The study provided a promising method to fabricate high-efficiency photocatalysts in water purification.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Key Laboratory of Groundwater Resources & Environment, Ministry of Education, Jilin University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2018.07.025.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
